title: QBE wary of property insurance rivalry, conflict risk
full_text:
America’s hot crop insurance market has buoyed QBE, but the Australian commercial insurer has pointed to growing competition in property as likely to keep a lid on its premium growth.
In a first-quarter update, QBE told investors it had increased premiums and warned they would rise in the “mid-single” digits in the year ahead.
QBE said its premiums were now 11 per cent higher than they were 12 months earlier. Excluding currency fluctuations, QBE said its premiums were about 7 per cent higher than last year.
While QBE is an Australian insurer, it writes nearly two-thirds of its business outside the country with major operations in the US and Europe.
QBE also revealed to shareholders it was exposed to the chaos in the Strait of Hormuz, where Iranian and US attacks have shut the shipping channel. The conflict in the Middle East is expected to result in about $US60m ($83m) in claims losses.
QBE gave its quarterly update as it prepared to hold its annual general meeting in Sydney. The insurer told investors it was confident in sustaining performance “over the medium term”.
But ruling off its first-quarter update, QBE told investors that competitive pressures were still present across the global insurance market, particularly in commercial property and Lloyds segments.
Excluding price rises, QBE still recorded a 6 per cent premium increase with its North American crop insurance business, while some international arms powered its performance.
Outside of its crop business, QBE’s premium growth rate, excluding price rises, was just 2 per cent year on year.
In his AGM speech, QBE chief executive Andrew Horton said the insurer was facing an environment “shaped by geopolitical uncertainty, major loss events and rapidly evolving risks”. But he said QBE was tracking ahead of its plan to keep losses below returns, with this currently at a combined operating ratio of about 92.5 per cent.
“We entered the year with momentum, a strong balance sheet and a clear strategy and, looking ahead, the outlook remains constructive,” he said.
He said the insurance market remained “broadly supportive”, with policies still delivering “favourable” increases across all the insurer’s global markets. “Our underwriting performance has been excellent, notwithstanding the growing geopolitical instability across the world,” Mr Horton said.
Catastrophe losses were at about $US300m ($416m) for the four months to April 30. QBE has budgeted $US517m to cover losses for the first half of the financial year.
Activist investors pressured QBE on Friday to improve its climate disclosures, in a move opposed by the insurer’s board and outgoing chair Mike Wilkins.
Mr Wilkins said a push to amend the insurer’s constitution to force climate disclosures showed “a fundamental lack of understanding of insurance”.
The motion was defeated.
QBE shares fell 1.55 per cent to $22.30, despite analysts saying the update was positive. Citi analyst Nigel Pittaway said premium growth was strong over the quarter and were “in line with QBE’s prior expectations”.
summary: QBE reports increased premiums amid competitive pressures in property insurance and geopolitical risks impacting its operations.

heading: General Insurance
key_points: QBE's premiums rose 11% year-on-year, with a forecast of mid-single digit increases ahead.
The insurer faces significant claims losses due to geopolitical tensions, particularly in the Strait of Hormuz.
Activist investors pushed for improved climate disclosures, which were opposed by QBE's board.
relevance: Understanding QBE's performance and market challenges is crucial for actuaries and financial professionals involved in risk assessment and insurance pricing.
